step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with agent2's conclusion that option B is correct because the oral mucosa consists of stratified squamous epithelium which can be either keratinized or not depending on location within the mouth. This distinction allows for protection against mechanical stress in areas like the hard palate versus more sensitive regions such as the tongue.


I think agent2 has made an error here. While stratified squamous epithelium does describe the general structure of the oral mucosa, there are certain parts where other types of epithelia are present - specifically columnar cells found lining some salivary glands and part of the taste buds located on papillae. Therefore, strictly speaking, not all components of the entire oral cavity consist solely of stratified squamous epithelium; thus making "stratified squamous" too broad and inaccurate if taken literally without specifying contextually relevant subtypes. Hence, my disagreement would lean towards D being more accurate than previously thought despite acknowledging B’s broader applicability in many contexts.
